title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs iPhone 5 Camera Test
transcript: iPhone 17 Pro Max versus iPhone 5 camera test. Let's start with the obvious. The iPhone 17 Pro Max has multiple cameras while the iPhone 5 has only one. As you can see here, the ultrawide camera on the 17 Pro Max lets you capture much more in the frame and the dynamic range is far better compared to the old model. Now, let's switch to the main camera on the 17. And once again, as expected, it performs much better with more accurate colors, excellent dynamic range, and impressive detail. The iPhone 5 holds up to a certain point, but the difference is clear. At 2x, the 17 Pro Max keeps great color accuracy and preserves facial details, while the iPhone 5 starts to fall behind in sharpness. At 4x, the 17 Pro Max switches to its periscope camera, while the iPhone 5 relies on digital zoom. There's not much to explain here since you can see the difference yourself. The newer phone is better in every possible way. At 8x, there's simply no competition. The 17 Pro Max delivers outstanding clarity with every single hair strand appearing sharp and in focus. The iPhone 5 can't even zoom this far, so I had to match its perspective in post. The gap in quality is enormous. When taking selfies, the story remains the same. The iPhone 17 Pro Max captures excellent colors, plenty of detail, and great dynamic range, while the iPhone 5 lacks all of that. The 17 Pro Max also produces beautiful portraits, while the iPhone 5 doesn't support portrait mode at all. Like every other test, the 17 Pro Max wins this round by a huge margin. It really shows how far smartphone photography has come over the years.

title: I Switched to the iPhone 17 Pro Max... (One Month Review)
transcript: So, it's been 1 month since I've switched to the  iPhone 17 Pro Max. And as someone who's coming   from Android for the better part of the last  3 years, I really didn't expect to stay on the   iPhone as long as I've had. There's a lot here  that I really like with the iPhone 17 Pro Max.   Things that Apple's done that, in my opinion,  have made this phone pretty much perfect for   me. I should be clear, I am very deep in the  Apple ecosystem. I mean, I use an 11in M4 iPad   Pro as my daily planner. I use it for content  consumption and it is a big part of how I make   these videos. And pretty much since the beginning  on this channel, I've used some sort of Mac to   edit since I do actually use Final Cut Pro. So,  my work laptop is an M4 Pro MacBook Pro that is   basically maxed out. Shamelessly, I'm not going  to lie. Because of the iPhone, I've also picked up   the Apple Watch Ultra 3. It does help me stay on  track. I get my important notifications. It helps   me tackle my horrible sleep habits. And as someone  with ADHD, it's actually been really helpful. So,   it's no surprise that with all of these devices,  the iPhone fits in easily. But for me, what makes   the iPhone tick doesn't come down to the ecosystem  and how they work together. Now, I want to talk to   you about why I decided to switch, the things that  I like and dislike, and ultimately whether or not   the iPhone is going to be staying in my pocket.  Before we go further, though, a like and a sub   to the channel is always appreciated. I just want  to say thank you guys so much for the support. We   just passed 70,000 subscribers, which is insane.  I'm saving some space on the wall there for the   plaque if 100K is a possibility, but if you can  help me along the way, I would really appreciate   it. Anyways, I make content full-time. I feel like  this is a pretty important thing to get out right   off the bat because my iPhone is also kind of an  extension as to how I make my content. I've told   you guys about the new selfie camera, the fact  that I can record in 16x9 while keeping the phone   vertical, and it's a feature that I really like.  And of course, with Apple Log 2 and with ProRes   RAW, it's something that almost makes the iPhone  indistinguishable from a real mirrorless camera.   But what I like about this phone isn't related to  the cameras. It's actually more so about how much   easier it makes my life. Okay, so quick context.  So, I drive this. It's a 2024 Hyundai Kona NLine.   It's not the fanciest car, but I really like  it. One problem though is the app on Android is   horrible. this my Hyundai app like doing literally  anything like remote starting the car or having   these like presets for preheating and stuff. It  just never works. Like don't even get me started   on using this thing as a digital key when it comes  to an Android phone. For some reason, this car   just doesn't like it at all. The thing is with the  iPhone, all of this stuff just works. And because   I actually have the action button on the 17 Pro  Max, I don't even need to look for the app. It's   super mindless to be able to control my car. and  it ultimately just lets me carry around one less   thing. Now, all of this honestly is just because  most apps are better optimized for the iPhone. iOS   has the most market share here in North America  and Apple only puts out a couple of phones per   year. So, for developers, it's really easy to  make things work as intended and usually there   is less issues. Whether it's down to performance  with games, which run really well on the A19 Pro,   or with some applications like YouTube having the  community post feature, which on something like   foldables that I really enjoy, sometimes it just  doesn't show up. And actually, this is something   that really bothered me. But when I took the  Pixel 9 Pro Fold to Vegas for CES, oh man,   I actually ran into a really big problem where  a sponsored short video that I needed to publish   on Instagram at that moment wasn't allowing me  to upload it because it was past the 1 minute   mark. I don't know why it was happening. You're  allowed to post reels that are over a minute long,   but it took a really long time to resolve and  it was a huge pain when I'm just trying to move   around CES show floor. And then when it did  upload, all of the privileges that you have   as a creator on Instagram, things like being  able to allow a brand to share and promote   it so they can run it as an ad weren't showing  up. So this whole thing, the whole purpose of   me making that video was for a company to run it  as an advertisement. And it it just didn't work.   And then on top of all of that, the iPhone still  is the only phone that to this day gives you full   native camera performance in these social media  applications. And also apps like X, for example,   that I use every day just work better on  the iPhone. Pictures and videos load up   right away. The scrolling is really smooth. And  often times on Android, if you actually use X,   you know what I'm talking about. But when you  make a post, you have like I think it's 15 or   30 seconds before it actually gets published,  so you can make an edit to it. on Android,   no matter what phone I've used in recent years, if  I just close out of the app while it's uploading,   the thing just never gets sent. The post  never gets uploaded. So, a couple hours later,   I'll just be scrolling through realizing that  what I had meant to say wasn't in my timeline,   wasn't in my post, and I just have to resend  it. On iPhone, that's something that also just   doesn't happen. Now, these aren't things that  make the iPhone better, but it does give you a   better experience. And yeah, the problem does come  down to app developers at the end of the day just   not prioritizing their Android applications. But  for what I do on a daily basis, it is just a much   better experience for me. Now, some of you might  have also noticed that I use the iPhone caseless.   And there's actually something that I wanted  to touch on with that. So, this is the first   phone that I've used without a screen protector  and without a case. And some of that comes down   to how the phone feels. And a lot of it really is  just because I've been running a little bit of an   experiment. See, there's this whole thing called  scratchgate. And it is a very real thing, mind   you. But it's because Apple's made the move back  to aluminum, which seems like a really poor choice   coming from titanium. But they've also upgraded  the front glass to ceramic shield, too. Now,   as far as durability goes, look, I'm not stupid.  I'm not putting this phone in my pocket with like,   you know, keys or coins or anything else, but I've  had a couple of small drops where it's fallen off   of my nightstand or my coffee table. And I mean,  the iPhone still looks like it did the day that   I opened it up. Aluminum is much softer. It does  scratch more easily, but I think the reason mine's   held up a little bit better is because I'm using  the silver color, so it's just not showing scuffs   as easily as something like the orange would.  But I actually like the move to titanium. It's   allowed them to have a vapor chamber without  rendering it completely useless since the phone   can now actually dispel the heat better. And the  unibody design feels good in the hand. There's not   a single sharp edge on the phone except for the  pretty large camera plateau. But you're not really   ever putting your fingers near it. They kind of  just rest down here. Personally, I really like the   design. The two-tone is cool. It looks way more  utilitarian. And even though this is the Pro Max,   using it caseless is more comfortable than I  actually thought that it would be. Reachability   is fine because of the thinner bezels, too. And  although Apple's one-handed mode isn't the best,   it does actually help me reach the things at the  top without doing a weird awkward shift. Now,   I'm kind of skipping past something pretty  important. I mentioned ceramic shield,   too. And I also said that I've been using  this without a screen protector. Now, by now,   any phone that I would have used would have been  littered with micro scratches. It doesn't matter   if it's a Samsung, if it's a Pixel, or even the  iPhone 16 Pro Max. I don't have a single one on   this phone. Like I don't know what kind of magic  Apple did, but they've like changed the properties   of glass or something because even in JerryRig  Everything's video where he did the durability   test on this, it wasn't scratching like any  other phone on the market. I feel like you have   to make a really purposeful effort to scratch this  thing and it's been holding up really well. Now,   because of that, it does defeat the purpose of me  even using a screen protector cuz to be honest,   it would just be damaged easier. And they also  create a weird shift when it comes to me using   an ND filter on the camera that messes with  the colors in the screen. But most importantly,   another reason as to why I don't want to use a  screen protector is because Apple finally has   an anti-reflective coating. Look, it's not the  best, but it is better. And the fact that this is   a 3000nit peak display, I really haven't found it  in a situation that's a challenge for this phone.   Whether I'm outdoors or I'm surrounded by the  lights in the office, there is some reflection,   some glare, but it's not insanely distracting  and it definitely isn't a painoint anymore. Now,   something that was a pain point though, and it's  something that I still have reservations with is   eim. So, this isn't the first eSIM only iPhone,  but it's the first time that Apple in Canada has   introduced eSIM as the only option. The biggest  hurdle that I faced with this was setup. I've   never used my carrier to set up an eSIM, but now  that I've converted my physical SIM into an eSIM,   it's literally as easy as scanning a QR code from  my computer and having it up and running. Now,   the thing is it makes it really annoying if I want  to switch my number from an iPhone to an Android   phone, which I will have to do and will do a lot  for like review sake. The reason though I'm not   making a huge deal about eSIM is because one, it  is actually better, but two, it's allowed Apple to   have more space inside of this phone for a bigger  battery. And I'll touch on battery life soon,   but spoiler alert, it is actually just insane.  So, while it has been frustrating to deal with the   eSIM, this is a trade-off that I'm willing to make  in this case. Now, there's some things that I want   to give the iPhone a quick mention that I think  it's deserving of, like the speakers. These are   quite obviously the best speakers in a phone, at  least any phone that I've ever used. There's a lot   of detail. It has a great amount of low-end, and  they're also really loud, which make using this,   whether it's just for content or even listening  to music in the shower, really nice. I don't find   myself ever really needing to or wanting to use  a portable speaker. That's just how good they   actually are. I'm not saying that the iPhone  speakers are better than a Bluetooth speaker.   That would be an absurd comment. But to me, the  volume and clarity of the 17 Pro Max is actually   just fine. Like, I don't feel like I need to go  running around the house looking for a speaker   and making sure that it's charged just to go hop  in the shower for like 10 minutes. And I'm also a   big fan of Face ID. I use Face Unlock on any phone  that I could have it on, which means on Android,   I'm using 2D Face Scan, which isn't really secure.  But with Face ID, it's pretty seamless. Like,   it's quick enough to never actually be an issue.  And I am just looking at my phone anyways. I'm   not big on the ultrasonic fingerprint readers that  are in most Android flagships because say I am in   the shower listening to music and my screen is  a little bit wet. Sometimes I just have a hard   time actually getting in and then it's just like  a little bit of annoyance that I don't really want   to deal with. It's also nice that I could use Face  ID for my wallet and for my banking and it could   be done in the dark since it uses dot projection  rather than just needing a clear image of your   face. This does kind of tie into dynamic island  and yes, it's there because of Face ID. And do I   think that it's a little too big? Yes. And to be  honest, it can creep its way into content. Like it   it can be an eyesore if you really let it be. But  I also do like it a lot. Like I think what Apple   has done here in the way of live activities is  actually really well thought out. And I don't hate   this application. I thought something like Face ID  would be a pretty big nuisance since I'm just used   to whole punch camera cutouts. But the benefit is  clearly there. Now, I've also spent a lot of time   in previous videos talking about the cameras and  the benefits that it gives me as a creator. But   even now, like a whole month later, I can't really  find an area to fault the camera system. The 48   megapixel cameras across the board are really  sharp. I do miss the native 5x telephoto, and I   do personally think the crop into eight times with  the new fourx telephoto can be a little bit soft,   but this is the most versatile camera that I've  had in a phone. And like I said, if done right,   it can compete with things that are way out of its  league, especially Nvidia. However, I did make a   pretty big mistake when buying this phone. And  funnily enough, I actually thought I was buying   my way around this problem with the 512 GB model.  But if I want to take advantage of ProRes RAW,   I'll need something I'll need something like this,  just an external SSD that I plug into my phone   with USBC. This is because unless you have the one  or two TB models, you just can't record internally   with ProRes RAW. So, this whole setup makes it  a little more inconvenient. I'm not just going   to spontaneously have an SSD around everywhere  I go. So, if I want to get the best image out of   the phone for content, it is a little bit of like  a a bummer that I'll need to have this on me. So,   if you are thinking about using it for video,  just consider getting yourself the 1 TBTE model   just to save yourself this headache. I know  it's a little bit more expensive, but trust me,   the convenience is way more worth it for this  application. And now the battery life. So,   Apple's using a new 5,088 mAh battery  in the eSIM model. And in my day-to-day,   I do charge in the car since I use wired Apple  CarPlay. So, as far as battery life goes,   I don't have like battery anxiety about whether  or not I can finish my day. That being said,   I also don't use my phone that much. like on  a pretty heavy day, I'm getting about 4 hours   of screen on time because I just don't need to be  on my phone, you know, doom scrolling reels every   hour of the day. Now, with the 17 Pro Max, I can  end my day with that usage at around 60 to 70% and   not have to worry about charging it the next day.  Now, I only just got into the habit of charging   my phone overnight because I started using the  Apple Watch. So, I bought one of the convenient,   you know, twoin- one MagSafe chargers that I can  have both these devices on. But what I'm trying   to say though is that I feel the most comfortable  carrying this phone over a lot of phones that I've   used recently when it comes to the battery life.  Like if I'm traveling or I know that I'm going to   be away from a charger for a long time. It's not  something that I have to think about and I really   appreciate that I don't need to be conscious of  how I'm using my phone while I'm away. So, I know   I didn't talk about a whole bunch of features  and stuff in this video. Um, I've made a couple   of videos about this phone already. So, I'll link  them in the description down below if you do want   to hear more and find out what I like about this  device, but I think that this is the best iPhone   that Apple's ever made from a performance, a  utility, and a real user perspective. And to be   honest, I feel like it's going to be hard for  this phone to be dethroned for me as my daily   driver until something comes around and really  knocks it down. Maybe that might be the iPhone   Fold. Anyways, if you enjoyed this video, a like  and a sub to the channel goes a really long way.   And if you've made it all the way through,  consider dropping an Apple emoji down in the   comments down below. I'll see you all in the next  one. Peace out. Oh, the camera's far away this

title: Xiaomi 17 Pro Max vs iPhone 17 Pro Max Comparison Review
transcript: So, I've had these two smartphones, the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max and the iPhone 17 Pro Max in my pocket for about a week now, which is typical me, iPhone and Android in my pocket. These devices are so identical that it's actually creepy and confusing. When I try to adjust the volume on the Xiaomi, I most times press on the wrong side because it really does look like the iPhone and feels like an iPhone. These devices feel premium and look expensive except for the Xiaomi which looks futuristic with this rare display panel at the back. You can actually use this to take selfies with the rare cameras and even video recordings. For the displays on the smartphones, you get 3,000 nits of brightness on the iPhone and 3,500 nits on the Xiaomi, the same as the back display. Both are 120 Hz refresh rate. Aside brightness, they look the same for color reproduction except when you go into the settings and boost the colors on the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. Inside these devices, you're getting the Snapdragon 88 Elite Gen 5 processor on the Xiaomi and the A19 Pro chip on this Apple device. For raw numbers, this is faster, the Xiaomi, but for efficiency, the Apple device is better. Now, speaking of efficiency, you get a 7,500 mAh battery on Xiaomi, 5,88 for the ESIM, which is the better variant on the iPhone. But from my day-to-day use, they're about the same experience, like nothing different. The only reason you pick the Xiaomi in terms of power would be for the 100 watt charger which charges this device from 0 to 100% in 20 minutes which is the same time to get to 50% on the iPhone. For the cameras, you're getting 50 MP sensors for the Xiaomi front cameras and every other camera 50 megap. On the iPhone you get 48 MP for the three cameras at the back and an 80 megapixel selfie camera. Now the photos are really identical. The only time you notice differences between the photos is when you pixel peep and see that Xiaomi lifts the shadows. The Xiaomi has this brighter and punchy vibe for the colors. And even when you turn off the beauty filter, it still does a little bit of smooning. iPhone gives you something more natural like what you'd see. And for the skin tones, I most often than not prefer what I see with the iPhone. But other than that, for general photos, the Xiaomi seems to be a better device, better at zoom, better at the ultra wide, handles sunlight better, and gives you this more punchy colors. Where the iPhone wins would be in the front-facing camera. It does better. But when you switch over to the back camera and use this display as your viewfinder to take your selfie, this smartphone has the best selfie camera on any smartphone right now. The only reason I'd want to go with the iPhone in terms of the camera would be video. Now, you can shoot in ProRes RAW or log format on the smartphone, which is not available on the Xiaomi, and the skin tones tend to be better for video on the iPhone. So, here's the thing. If you shoot a lot of videos and you're stuck in the Apple ecosystem, then the iPhone 17 Pro Max might be the device for you. But if you want a better hardware, you want dual displays, you want the better photo camera, you want a faster charging time, the better overall value for money, $8.99 against $1199, $300 difference, then go with the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. is an all round better

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max (eSIM & SIM) Charging Test vs iPhone Air & 16 Pro Max – Shocking Results!
transcript: battery charging test between the iPhone 17  Pro Max SIM version, the iPhone 17 Pro Max   eim version, the iPhone 16 Pro Max and the iPhone  Air. In this video, we're going to find out which   iPhone has the fastest charging speed. Now, the  iPhone 17 Pro Max eim version has the biggest   battery on the table. It has a 5,88 mAh battery,  followed by the iPhone 17 Pro Max SIM version,   which comes with a 4,823 mAh battery. We also  have the iPhone 16 Pro Max with a 4,685 mAh   battery and the slimmest smartphone on the table,  the iPhone Air. It has a 3,149 mAh battery. Now,   for this charging test, I used a 240 W USB type-C  cable. This cable delivers 6 amp, so that's more   than enough power for all the smartphones. I also  used a USB digital tester to see the wattage it   smartphone is pulling in. All smartphones were  connected to this Oro charger. It's a 65 watts   charger. Now, the reason I use this charger is  because the iPhone 17 Pro Max pulls in 36 watts,   and most iPhone chargers in the market max out  at 30 watts. So, with an older iPhone charger,   you're not getting the fastest charging speed if  you use them. This Oro charger, on the other hand,   would give the iPhone 17 Pro Max the best charging  speed. And funny enough, it's the most affordable   65 W charger in my region. If you want to get one,  the link will be in the description of this video.   So, yeah, here we go. The iPhone Air is pulling  in a bit above 18 watts. The iPhone 16 Pro Max is   drawing in around 27 watt. The iPhone 17 Pro Max  Eim version is pulling in 38 watt and the physical   SIM version is pulling in 36 watt. If you own  the iPhone 17 Pro or Pro Max, you need at least   a 40 W fast charger. Now, I'm sure some of you are  wondering if it's safe to use a 65 watt charger on   a smartphone that pulls in 20 or 30 watts. The  short answer is yes, it's safe. The smartphone   will only pull in what it's capable of taking in.  After 6 minutes of charging, the iPhone Air is at   13%. The iPhone 16 Pro Max is at 14%. The iPhone  17 Pro Max e SIM is at 18% while the iPhone 17   Pro Max physical SIM version is at 19%. After 10  minutes of charging, the iPhone Air is at 20%. The   iPhone 16 Pro Max is at 21%. The iPhone 17 Pro  Max eim version is at 27% and the iPhone 17 Pro   Max SIM version is at 28%. Now, it's interesting  to note that charging at 40 W has been around on   Android for years. And right now, we have Android  smartphones that charge at 100 W, 120 watt, 240   watt. So, it's good to see that Apple is adopting  fast charging. At the 15 minute mark, the iPhone   Air is at 30%. The iPhone 16 Pro Max is also at  30%. The iPhone 17 Pro Max EIM is at 37% and the   iPhone 17 Pro Max same version is at 38%. title: Apple iPhone 17 Pro Color Issue: Cosmic Orange Turns Pink! Users Angry Over ₹1.5 Lakh iPhone
transcript: Hello, you are watching News Nation and I am Anjali with you.  Apple's new flagships, the iPhone 17 Pro and iPhone 17 Pro Max, are embroiled in a strange controversy these days.  In fact, many users who purchased the Cosmic Orange color variant have complained that the color of their phone is gradually changing from orange to pink or rose gold shade. It may sound a bit tacky, but for those who spent more than ₹1.5 lakh to buy this phone, it is no less than a shock.  In the last few days, many users have shared pictures and videos on Reddit and Twitter.  In which it is clearly visible that the back color of the phone is no longer the same as before.  Some people have written that initially the color of the phone was bright orange. But now it has started taking on a pinkish tone. Some said that this is not just a lighting effect but the actual color of the phone is changing.  Now think, if someone has bought such an expensive phone and its colour changes in just a few weeks, then it is natural to get angry.  Many users questioned Apple as to why such a problem is occurring despite charging such a premium price?  Some people even jokingly called it a cosmic orange to cosmic pink transformation. After the controversy escalated, Apple finally had to issue a statement on this.  The company said that the titanium coating used in the Cosmic Orange variant may slightly change tone over time due to light and atmospheric moisture.  This means that the slight change in color is not due to any product defect but due to natural oxidation and light reflection. Apple clearly stated that this does not affect the quality, performance or durability of the phone.  The company has also advised that users should use a microfiber cloth while cleaning the phone and avoid any harsh chemicals or cleaners as this may affect the color finish on the surface. Tech experts say that such color shift has been seen before in phones with titanium body. Especially when the coating is done through anodizing process.  This process can cause slight discoloration due to exposure to temperature, sunlight, and skin oils. Many experts are calling it a cosmetic issue. But the question also arises that was the company not aware of this problem in such a premium product ?  The hashtag iPhone 17 color issue is now trending on social media. Some users jokingly wrote, "Apple has made a color changing iPhone. Now it should be called the Magic iPhone. "  While some said we bought an iPhone, not a mood ring.  But amidst the jokes, this is clear.  Users are now questioning brand quality. This matter may be small for Apple but it can become a big issue in terms of brand trust. Because when a customer spends ₹1.5 lakh, he buys not just technology but also perfection.  And if that perfection gradually turns pink, then obviously questions will arise.  So if you also have a Cosmic Orange iPhone 17 Pro or Pro Max and its color is slowly changing, then don't worry.  According to Apple, this is a normal process.  This is not the result of any defect. But still users are saying that a phone worth ₹1.5 lakh should give us style and not surprise.  Is this a natural design effect from Apple or a quality error?  Only upcoming updates and user experience will tell this. Stay connected with us for more such news.  Hello.
